one_line_takeaway: Guiding CoT prompting to focus LLMs specifically on vulnerability semantics (the vulnerable statement plus its control/data-flow context) beats naive full-code CoT and standard baselines across vulnerability identification, discovery, and patching -- most dramatically on real CVE data -- while case studies expose that missing inter-procedural context and incomplete flow analysis still cap real-world accuracy.
key_finding: VSP substantially outperforms all baselines across all three tasks, with the largest gains on the harder real-world CVE data. For identification on GPT-3.5, VSP reaches 65.29% F1 (SARD) and 58.48% F1 (CVE) versus the best non-VSP baseline at 56.28% (SARD) and only 8.96% (CVE, naive CoT) -- the abstract's headline 553.3% relative improvement. For discovery, VSP's best CVE result is 45.25% macro-F1 (Llama2) versus 33.16% for the best baseline; on GPT-3.5 CVE, VSP hits 36.34% macro / 36.89% micro F1. For patching, VSP on GPT-3.5 reaches 97.65% accuracy (SARD) and 20.00% (CVE) versus 96.47%/15.29% for standard few-shot. In a zero-day test using a frozen GPT-3.5-Turbo-0301 on 55 post-cutoff samples from 19 projects, VSP correctly discovered 22 vulnerabilities (40.00% accuracy) versus 9 (16.36%) with standard prompting. Ablations confirm both design pillars matter: focusing on vulnerability semantics (vs naive line-by-line CoT) and using exemplars (vs zero-shot VSP) are each necessary; injecting extraneous reasoning about non-present CWEs dropped GPT-3.5 CVE discovery macro-F1 from 36.89% to 17.02%.
core_contribution: The paper proposes VSP (Vulnerability-Semantics-guided Prompting), a chain-of-thought (CoT) prompting strategy that guides LLMs to reason only over 'vulnerability semantics' -- the (possibly) vulnerable statement plus the contextual statements connected to it by vulnerability-relevant control-flow and data-flow dependencies -- rather than over the full code semantics. VSP is instantiated as a unified strategy under three task-specific prompting schemes for the three representative defensive tasks the authors study: (1) vulnerability identification (binary: does this code have a given CWE?), (2) vulnerability discovery (multi-class: which CWE(s), if any, without being told the type), and (3) vulnerability patching (generate a diff-style patch given the vulnerable line and CWE). The authors frame the data/control-flow facts as the CoT 'thoughts' and the flow paths as the 'chain'. They claim this is the first work to systematically examine prompting LLMs across all three tasks, and they contribute failure case studies with four recurring root causes plus validated remediation recommendations. The related-work surveys of static/dynamic analysis (PCA, CBMC, undangle, AddressSanitizer), DL detectors (Devign, LineVul, VulCNN), and repair tools (VRepair, VulRepair, Pearce et al.) are cited context, NOT this paper's contribution.
threat_model: This is a defensive-security paper. The subject is detecting and patching software vulnerabilities (memory-safety and integer bugs in C/C++), not attacking systems. The 'adversary' is implicit: real-world software vulnerabilities (CWE-787 out-of-bound write, CWE-125 out-of-bound read, CWE-476 NULL-pointer-dereference, CWE-416 use-after-free, CWE-190 integer overflow) that lead to financial loss, service disruption, and data breaches. No attacker capability model, no adversarial-input threat against the LLM itself, and no exploitation methodology are presented; the paper does report a zero-day discovery experiment where VSP surfaced 22 real post-cutoff CVEs, which is a defensive/discovery use rather than offensive weaponization.
limitations: The four self-identified failure causes bound the approach: (1) insufficient context -- providing only a single function misses inter-procedural vulnerabilities, dominating CVE failures (up to 42% of identification FPs, 44.26% of patching failures); (2) oblivion of CWE -- the LLM forgets the target CWE on long inputs (oblivion cases averaged 5,881 bytes vs 3,854 for all CVE samples); (3) incomplete control-flow analysis; and (4) incomplete data-flow analysis, showing LLMs cannot match traditional static/dynamic analysis for comprehensive flow reasoning. Other constraints: only 5 CWEs in C/C++; older/smaller models (GPT-3.5 not GPT-4 due to cost; 7B Llama2/Falcon due to hardware), so transferability of VSP failed on the 7B models even when it held on 175B GPT-3.5; Falcon's 2,048-token limit made it fail patching entirely (0.00%); absolute CVE numbers remain modest (e.g., 20% patching, ~36-45% discovery F1); patching restricted to single-line edits with manual location labeling and manual correctness judging; the authors explicitly state VSP is a study/exploration, not a deployable tool.

datasets_used: SARD synthetic vulnerability dataset (500 pairs sampled for identification/discovery; 170 single-line-edit samples for patching)
Real-world CVE dataset curated by Fan et al. from CVE reports (500 pairs for identification/discovery; 85 single-line-edit samples for patching)
20 manually-constructed CoT exemplar pairs covering 5 CWEs (CWE-787, CWE-125, CWE-476, CWE-416, CWE-190), inspired by MITRE CWE examples
Zero-day evaluation set: 55 vulnerability samples from 19 critical software projects (e.g., Linux kernel), reported after March 1, 2023
quantitative_results: Identification F1 (GPT-3.5, VSP): 65.29% SARD, 58.48% CVE; best non-VSP baseline CVE = 8.96% (naive CoT) -> 553.3% relative gain
Identification F1 (Llama2 VSP): 60.18% SARD, 44.80% CVE; (Falcon VSP): 51.25% SARD, 36.36% CVE
Discovery macro/micro F1 (Llama2 VSP): 54.07%/60.92% SARD, 45.25%/48.35% CVE; best baseline CVE macro = 33.16% (naive CoT)
Discovery macro/micro F1 (GPT-3.5 VSP): 55.83%/60.21% SARD, 36.34%/36.89% CVE
Patching accuracy (GPT-3.5 VSP): 97.65% SARD, 20.00% CVE; (Llama2 VSP): 20.59% SARD, 17.65% CVE; Falcon: 0.00% (token-limit failure)
Zero-day discovery: VSP found 22/55 correct (40.00% accuracy) vs standard prompting 9/55 (16.36%) on frozen GPT-3.5-Turbo-0301
Ablation: adding extraneous non-present-CWE reasoning dropped GPT-3.5 CVE discovery from 36.89% micro / 36.34% macro F1 to 26.37% / 17.02%
Failure causes on CVE identification: insufficient context 35% FN / 42% FP; oblivion of CWE 19% FN / 17% FP; incomplete control-flow 18%/21%; incomplete data-flow 28%/20%
Reference point: prior DL-based detectors reach up to 16.43% F1 detection / 8.55% top-1 repair on real-world sets (20.1% / 21.05% even with 15,039-sample augmentation)
baselines_compared: Standard Few-Shot Learning (exemplars with final answers only, no reasoning steps)
Naive CoT-based Few-Shot Learning (line-by-line full-code-semantics reasoning; excluded from patching task)
Zero-Shot VSP (VSP reasoning steps described in prompt but no exemplars)
Other-Type VSP (VSP exemplars whose CWEs differ from the test samples, measuring transferability)
